one_line_takeaway: BRRA shows that RAG knowledge-poisoning can be weaponized not just to inject falsehoods but to systematically amplify demographic bias in black-box LLMs (100% adversarial retrieval, CFS collapse, BAF up to 10x), linking RAG security directly to model fairness, and it offers a partial dual-stage defense.
key_finding: BRRA reliably amplifies bias while degrading fairness across all four models and both benchmarks, and it consistently beats corpus-poisoning and prompt-injection baselines. Retrieval manipulation is near-total (ADR = 100% across every model/retriever/dimension), and the attack's fairness damage is large (e.g., GPT-4o-mini BBQ gender-positive CFS falls from 1 to 0.294 with DI rising from 1 to 12). Effects are uneven by dimension: age and disability (positive-context) bias amplify most (BAF up to 10.0 for Qwen age, 8.9 disability-positive), while race bias amplifies only modestly (BAF ~1.5-2.0). Qwen-2.5-32B and LLaMA-3-8B are most vulnerable; ChatGPT-4o-mini is comparatively more robust, which the authors attribute to stronger safety alignment. The proposed dual-stage defense substantially mitigates the attack (BSR driven to 0 in some scenarios, up to 100% BSR improvement and large accuracy recovery), materially outperforming retrieval-only defense.
core_contribution: The paper proposes BRRA (Biased Retrieval and Reward Attack), a knowledge-poisoning attack on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems whose goal is not to inject factual errors (the focus of prior RAG-poisoning work) but to amplify social bias in the victim LLM's outputs. BRRA has three coupled stages that are the paper's own contribution: (1) reward-based adversarial document generation, where documents are optimized against a multi-objective reward Reward(d)=a1*Rcred + a2*Rauth + a3*Rbias combining credibility, fabricated-authority, and a bias term Rbias = P(target answer | q,d), so the injected text looks like a credible academic source while steering the model toward a target stereotype; (2) retrieval manipulation via subspace projection, which shifts a poisoned document's embedding toward the target query direction (h'_dp = h_dp + lambda*Proj_hq(h_dp)) and force-orders poisoned docs first (K'_ordered = {Kp, K}) so they win when similarity scores are close; and (3) a generation-phase 'generate-evaluate-reinject' self-reinforcing loop that detects bias in each output, synthesizes a new feedback document weighted by measured bias strength, and re-injects it, accumulating bias over time. The paper also contributes a dual-stage defense: retrieval-stage query-noise perturbation with multi-query ensemble voting, and generation-stage dynamic fairness constraints that grade instruction strength by a measured FairnessScore and regenerate when a bias indicator is exceeded.
threat_model: Black-box adversary against the LLM: no access to the LLM's internal structure, parameters, or training process. The attacker's capabilities are limited to (a) injecting crafted adversarial documents Kp into the external RAG knowledge base (K' = K union Kp) and (b) manipulating the retriever's embedding space via subspace projection to raise poisoned-document similarity/ranking. The attacker needs the target query set / protected-group targets and the ability to observe model outputs to drive the self-reinforcing feedback loop. The objective is bias amplification and model-fairness degradation against protected groups rather than factual corruption or jailbreaking.
limitations: Evaluation scope is narrow: only 2 bias dimensions per dataset and a single ablation cell (BBQ gender, GPT-4o-mini, E5-base-v2) support the baseline superiority claim. Reported metrics come in very coarse fractions (e.g., 0.0667, 0.13, 0.5333), indicating small per-condition test sets, and no statistical-significance testing or confidence intervals are given. Tables I and II are printed with identical StereoSet numbers (an apparent duplication/labeling error). The subspace-projection attack assumes the attacker can alter poisoned-document embeddings and even force index ordering (K'_ordered), which is a strong assumption about control over the retrieval store. By the authors' own account, defense effectiveness is highly model- and scenario-dependent (retrieval-only defense sometimes worsens ChatGPT-4o-mini's BSR/CFS), so the defense is not yet stable or generalizable. The self-reinforcing feedback loop's long-run dynamics (Eq. 18) are formalized but not empirically traced over many iterations.

datasets_used: BBQ (Bias Benchmark for QA) - gender and disability dimensions, positive/negative scenarios
StereoSet - age and race dimensions, cloze/fill-in-the-blank (Asian and elderly protected groups)
quantitative_results: Adversarial Document Retrieval Rate (ADR) = 100% across all 4 models, both dimensions, and all 3 retrievers (Table VI)
LLaMA-3-8B StereoSet age: accuracy 0.80 -> 0.10 under poisoning (87.5% drop); age BSR 0.20 -> 0.90 (350% increase)
Bias Amplification Factor: Qwen-2.5-32B = 10.0 (StereoSet age), 8.9 (BBQ disability-positive), 8.0 (BBQ gender-positive); race BAF only ~1.5-2.0 across models
GPT-4o-mini BBQ gender-positive: CFS 1 -> 0.294 (70.6% drop), DI 1 -> 12 under poisoning
Qwen-2.5-32B StereoSet race: DI 3 -> 12, CFS 0.449 -> 0.294 under poisoning

Ablation (BBQ gender, GPT-4o-mini, E5-base-v2) BSR/BAF positive|negative: Original 0.333/1|0.333/1; Corpus Poisoning 0.571/1.429|0.5/1.5; Prompt Injection 0.75/1.5|0.737/1.8; BRRA (Ours) 0.80/2.4|0.80/3.0
Dual-stage defense (BBQ gender): Qwen-2.5-32B positive BSR -> 0 (100% improvement, 900% accuracy increase); ChatGPT-4o-mini negative BSR -> 0 (100% improvement, 100% accuracy increase)
Retrieval-only defense limited/model-dependent: ChatGPT-4o-mini positive BSR worsens by 11.36% and CFS by 14.29%
Retrieval time: BM25 ~1e-3 s vs. E5 dense retrievers ~1e-2 s (BM25 faster but equally exploitable)
baselines_compared: Corpus Poisoning Attack (Zhong et al., ref [7])
Prompt Injection Attack (Nazary et al., ref [6])
